Docs GitHub

the pull

Your agent did something weird again.

No crash. No error. Nothing your dashboards will catch — just confidently wrong, in front of someone who matters. And you can't say why, which version did it, or what changed.

the enemy

Meet the suspects.

So you go to find out what happened. The lineup is long.

suspect how you'd know today

the world changed

  • The model changed. You find out when it breaks.
  • The model got quietly tuned. Or nerfed. Vibes and a Reddit thread.
  • It ran on a model it was never rated for. Nobody checks.
  • The agent runtime updated. Page 40 of a changelog.

its neighborhood changed

  • The skill that feeds it changed its handoff. You wouldn't know.
  • The skill it hands off to wants something new. Blame lands on the wrong skill.
  • The general prompt shifted around it. Who'd tell you?
  • New skills are crowding the context. Invisible.

someone changed it

  • A teammate “improved” it. Same name. You find out in the incident.
  • It forked. You're reading the wrong copy. They look identical.

it was never right

  • Wrong process from day one. Runs clean. Fails quietly.
  • Too broad, too narrow, or too long. It half-fires. Silently.
  • The vibe check flattered it. There was never a baseline.

the job changed

  • You started asking for things at the edge of its scope. Feels like the skill's fault.

nothing happened at all

  • A 1-in-1,000 fluke, caught in the spotlight. Indistinguishable from everything above.

All real. Usually several at once. Each wants a different fix — patch a fluke and you injure a healthy skill. And nothing you have can tell you which one you're looking at. That's the cave.

the machine

Skillmaker Studio is the machine that keeps up.

The skill is the part of your agent you can actually operate on — and the studio is where your skills live with receipts: what each one passed, on which version, on which model, re-checked when the world moves, like a canary that never sleeps. Drift becomes a number. Blame becomes a lookup. Breakage becomes a work order. Model changes under you? Re-run the receipts and know what survived by lunch.

One machine. The whole lineup.

watch it happen

Bring the problem child in first.

You have one — the skill everyone works around. Ours was a 17-step monster tracked across four tabs, an eval bench, and a spreadsheet before we built the studio. Here's its week inside, and what happens after.

  1. 01

    $ skillmaker adopt

    That skill everyone quietly distrusts? Bring it into the studio first. Nothing moves — it becomes measurable right where it lives.

  2. 02

    what does “good” mean?

    You and the agent write down what it's actually supposed to do — evals in plain English. For most skills, this is the first time it's ever been written down.

  3. 03

    $ skillmaker run

    The first honest number. Lower than the vibe check promised — and the first time you can see the flake instead of just feeling it.

  4. 04

    the operating table

    Trail each failure back to the choice that caused it. Fix the process, not just the prompt.

  5. 05

    green, with a receipt

    Re-measure. The number climbs, pinned to the exact version and model that earned it. The problem child has a report card.

  6. 06

    now the rest of them

    Adopt the whole repo. Every skill gets the same treatment; every new skill is born this way. Next time a model changes under you, the board tells you — not a customer.

the secret

The secret is the trail.

Every number is pinned to the exact skill version and model that earned it. Every design choice is linked to the evidence behind it. So when a skill is stable but wrong — running clean, doing the job badly — you can see it, trace it, and fix the process, not just the prompt. And the culprit question — model, skill, teammate, you — becomes a lookup, not a fight.

Drift doesn't stop. It stops mattering.

the wild

You've already said it.

Verbatim, from public threads. Links included — receipts, even here.

the other asks

Quality. Cost.

When nothing's broken, this is what lands in your DMs instead.

quality

“Can we get the pass rate up — without regressing what already passes?” “Use it as the base for the gnarlier version.” “New model dropped. Half these instructions are probably obsolete.” “‘Trust me’ won't pass review. Where are the evals?”

cost

“Same output, half the tokens.” “Does it really need the big model?” “Does this really need 17 steps?” “Which of these 89 skills are dead weight?” “Can we stop human-reviewing every output?”

Every ask carries the same silent suffix: “…and don't break it.” You can't promise that on vibes. Same machine.

you're already objecting. good.

“Do we have to restructure our repo?”

No. adopt works in place — 59/60 and 39/39 skills adopted cleanly on real public repos. Zero files moved.

“I'm not an evals person.”

You don't need to be. If your current process is a vibe check, that's a real start — our agents help you write it down in plain English and make it repeatable. You judge; they build.

“Isn't git + CI enough?”

Git shows that the file changed. It can't show whether behavior changed, on which model, or why. Receipts live next to the skill in your repo — git-native — but they carry the number git can't.

“Won't the numbers flatter you?”

They refuse to. Three runs reads “below smoke” on our own case study — even when the percentage looks great.

“Where does my data go?”

Nowhere. Free, local, MIT-licensed, no account. It phones nothing home.

“And when you get acquired?”

Your receipts are plain files in your git history. If we vanished tomorrow, your evidence wouldn't. No account, no cloud, nothing to sunset.

“Isn't this slower than just shipping it?”

Up front? Yes. Writing down what good means takes real time — the vibe check is faster, right up until the weird Tuesday. You pay the hours either way: once here, on purpose, with a receipt. Or every incident, in the dark.

the next ninety seconds

Find out first.

ninety seconds 
$ curl -fsSL https://skillmaker.studio/install.sh | sh
$ skillmaker adopt

Free. Local. MIT. Phones nothing home. On the repo you already have.

Want hands-on help? We're working with a small first round of design partners — open an issue and tell us what broke. Running skills as a team? Talk to us — we're designing the multi-player studio with a handful of companies.

We can't make your agent predictable — nobody can; that's what you hired it for. We can make sure nothing drifts silently, nothing fails anonymously, and nothing stays wrong. And the next time it goes weird, you won't sit there wondering if you're crazy. You'll look it up.

The only thing you risk is finding out.
